summary.objective: Fix the highest-risk security weaknesses in authentication, token storage, SSRF handling, tenant isolation, and abuse controls.

finding.title: Shop impersonation is possible because embedded session tokens are decoded but never signature-verified, and an untrusted header is accepted as fallback identity

evidence.item: `authorization` bearer tokens are only base64-decoded and checked for `exp` and `dest`.
The code explicitly leaves JWT signature verification as TODO.
If no bearer token is accepted, `x-shopify-shop-domain` is trusted without cryptographic proof.

finding.impact: An attacker who can invoke server actions or otherwise send crafted requests can impersonate any `*.myshopify.com` shop, read tenant-scoped data, generate or approve fixes, and trigger Shopify Admin API actions using stored offline tokens for the impersonated tenant.
finding.exploitPath: Forge a JWT payload with a chosen `dest`, or omit the token and send `x-shopify-shop-domain: victim.myshopify.com`, then call authenticated server actions that rely on `requireVerifiedShop()`.

requiredFixes.fix: Replace custom token parsing with full Shopify session token verification using Shopify's supported verification utilities.
Remove the `x-shopify-shop-domain` trust fallback entirely unless it is tied to server-side session state.
Fail closed when verification cannot be completed.
Add tests proving forged bearer tokens and spoofed headers are rejected.

acceptanceCriteria.criterion: Requests with modified JWT payloads are rejected.
Requests that only carry `x-shopify-shop-domain` are rejected.
All server actions requiring tenant identity derive it from a verified Shopify session token or equivalent server-trusted session state.

finding.title: Offline Shopify access tokens are stored in plaintext in Supabase

evidence.item: The database schema defines `access_token TEXT NOT NULL` without envelope encryption or key management.

finding.impact: A database leak, SQL console misuse, misconfigured backups, or insider access would expose long-lived offline Admin API tokens and allow direct compromise of connected Shopify stores.

requiredFixes.fix: Encrypt access tokens at rest using envelope encryption backed by a server-side key management strategy.
Decrypt only at the point of Shopify client construction.
Minimize token logging and error propagation around session storage and retrieval.
Plan key rotation and token re-encryption support.

acceptanceCriteria.criterion: Database rows no longer contain usable plaintext Shopify tokens.
Shopify clients can still be constructed by server code after decrypting values in memory.
A migration path exists for already-stored plaintext tokens.

finding.title: Public URL auditing launches a full browser against arbitrary HTTPS targets without authentication or effective abuse controls

evidence.item: `runAccessibilityAudit(url)` is exported as a server action and can be triggered for arbitrary URLs.
The function launches Chromium, loads remote pages, waits for `networkidle`, and allows 30 second navigation timeouts.
There is no authentication requirement and no rate limit on this generic audit path.

finding.impact: Attackers can burn CPU, memory, browser slots, egress, and third-party API quotas by repeatedly submitting expensive audits, turning the service into a resource exhaustion target and an arbitrary external web fetcher.

requiredFixes.fix: Require authenticated tenancy for browser-based audits, or place the unauthenticated path behind strict IP/session-based rate limiting and quotas.
Cap concurrent browser launches and overall job duration.
Consider a queue/worker model rather than direct request-time execution.
Log and meter audit invocations by actor, not just by shop.

acceptanceCriteria.criterion: Unauthenticated callers cannot launch unlimited audits.
Repeated requests from the same actor are throttled consistently across instances.
The service maintains bounded browser concurrency.

finding.title: SSRF checks are vulnerable to DNS rebinding and TOCTOU because hostname validation is separate from the browser's actual network connection

evidence.item: `validateURL()` resolves DNS and approves the hostname before the browser makes the real request.
The approved IP is not pinned to the subsequent browser connection.
The request interceptor repeats the same hostname-based check, which still depends on fresh DNS resolution and does not bind the browser to the vetted address.

finding.impact: An attacker who controls DNS for a target hostname may pass validation with a public IP and later rebind the hostname to internal infrastructure or cloud metadata endpoints when Chromium performs the real fetch.

requiredFixes.fix: Resolve and pin approved addresses, or proxy outbound requests through a controlled allow/deny layer that evaluates the actual destination socket.
Reject domains with unstable or attacker-controlled resolution where practical.
Expand SSRF defenses to treat DNS rebinding as part of the threat model.

acceptanceCriteria.criterion: A hostname cannot pass validation and later connect to a different blocked IP.
Tests cover DNS rebinding or equivalent mocked address-swapping scenarios.

finding.title: Tenant isolation depends entirely on application code because RLS is disabled on security-sensitive tables

evidence.item: Server code relies on `shop` filters while using the service-role client that bypasses database protections.

finding.impact: Any future missed filter, auth bypass, debug endpoint, or accidental client exposure of privileged queries becomes a full cross-tenant data disclosure or write vulnerability because the database is not enforcing tenant boundaries.

requiredFixes.fix: Reintroduce RLS where feasible, with policies aligned to a trustworthy authenticated principal.
Add schema constraints such as `shop NOT NULL` where tenant binding is mandatory.
Reduce service-role usage to the smallest possible set of trusted server paths.

acceptanceCriteria.criterion: Cross-tenant access is denied even if an application query forgets the `shop` filter.
`audits` and `proposed_fixes` records cannot be inserted without a tenant binding.

finding.title: Rate limiting is in-memory and keyed to attacker-controlled values, so protections disappear across instances and are easy to evade

evidence.item: Serverless or horizontally scaled deployments will not share counters.
The OAuth limiter is keyed by `shop`, which is user-supplied and easy to rotate.

finding.impact: Abuse protections are weaker than they appear in production and can be bypassed by distributing requests across instances or varying the chosen shop key.

requiredFixes.fix: Move rate limiting to a shared store such as Redis, Upstash, or another centralized backend.
Key limits by actor identity or source IP plus route, not only by shop.
Apply limits consistently to OAuth, audits, AI generation, and any browser-launching paths.

acceptanceCriteria.criterion: Limits continue to hold under multi-instance deployment.
Rotating the `shop` query parameter does not bypass callback abuse protection.

phase.task: Fix EPS-001 before touching lower-severity issues because it undermines every tenant-scoped server action.
After auth is trustworthy, add regression tests for spoofed headers and forged tokens.
Implement token encryption at rest for Shopify sessions.
Lock down or meter public browser-based audit execution.
Strengthen SSRF protections against DNS rebinding.
Move tenant isolation and abuse controls closer to the database and shared infrastructure layers.
